text.title: EDA Antipatterns
text.summary: Critical EDA violations: event as command, oversized events, missing correlation ID, CRUD event names.

antipattern.description: Publisher names an event imperatively (ProcessPayment, SendEmail) and expects only one specific consumer to act on it.
antipattern.reason: Commands have exactly one intended handler. Using an event channel for a command couples the producer to the consumer's presence, defeating the decoupling purpose of EDA.

example.code: # BAD: sending a command disguised as an event await bus.publish("ProcessPayment", {"order_id": order_id, "amount": 99.99})

# GOOD: publish a fact; payment service subscribes to it await bus.publish("order.placed", {"order_id": order_id, "total": 99.99}) # payment-service subscribes to "order.placed" and charges independently

antipattern.description: Event payload contains full embedded objects rather than IDs. Example: OrderPlaced event contains the entire customer profile, full product catalog entries, and complete shipping address history.
antipattern.reason: Large events bloat the broker, slow consumers, make schema evolution harder, and couple producers to consumer data needs. Consumers that need full objects should fetch them separately via ID.

antipattern.description: Events do not carry a correlation_id or trace context. When a flow fails across 5 services and 3 brokers, there is no way to reconstruct the event chain.
antipattern.reason: Distributed tracing without correlation IDs requires log archaeology. A single missing correlation_id makes debugging a distributed transaction blind and time-consuming.

antipattern.description: Events named UserUpdated, OrderModified, ProductChanged — CRUD verbs applied to aggregate state.
antipattern.reason: CRUD events lose business intent. OrderModified could mean a shipping address change, an item added, or a cancellation — consumers cannot determine what to do without parsing the full payload.
